title: Extensible links in a broad class of single polymer chain models
authors: Michael R. Buche, Matthew J. Grasinger, Jason P. Mulderrig
abstract: The physics of polymer chains is often probed using molecular stretching experiments and various idealized single-chain models. The majority of these models consist of a discrete sequence of links, which may be treated as rigid or extensible. Although such models are well established and many specific extensible variants have been proposed, no generally applicable theory has been presented. Moreover, most existing treatments are heuristic rather than systematically and rigorously derived. This critical gap is closed here through the development of a generally applicable asymptotic theory for including link extensibility in a broad class of discrete models for single-chain thermodynamics. The theory is verified analytically using the freely jointed chain and validated numerically using the freely rotating chain. The resulting approximation is first-order accurate in inverse link stiffness, with quadratically decreasing error, and recovers extensible behavior across all link stiffnesses from a single rigid-link reference calculation.
summary: This paper develops a general, asymptotically correct theoretical framework for modeling single polymer chains whose links possess finite extensibility. By applying advanced mathematical techniques like Laplace's method, the authors derive accurate predictions for chain extension under force. This work provides a significant methodological advance by unifying treatments across different physical models.
main_problem: The primary goal is developing a generally applicable asymptotic theory to rigorously incorporate link extensibility into discrete single-chain models for calculating thermodynamics.
main_result: The developed theory provides an asymptotically accurate approximation for the longitudinal chain extension, $\gamma(\eta)$, that successfully reproduces known results across various polymer models.
method: The authors employ an asymptotic approach using Laplace's method on the partition function when link stiffness ($\kappa$) is large, yielding a systematic expansion in powers of $\kappa^{-1}$.
model_or_system: The study focuses on single polymer chains modeled as discrete sequences of links. The models include the freely jointed chain and the freely rotating chain, characterized by an energy potential involving link angles and individual link potentials.
key_observables: Nondimensional longitudinal chain extension ($\gamma(\eta)$) under an applied force ($\eta$).
important_parameters: Link stiffness ($\kappa$), which must be large ($\kappa \gg 1$) for the asymptotic theory to be valid.
main_assumptions: The theory is strictly asymptotic, requiring the link stiffness $\kappa$ to be much greater than one. The approximation builds upon an inextensible reference system.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the asymptotic approximation to the exact result for the freely jointed chain, showing rapid convergence with increasing $\kappa$. Figure 2 validates this theory using Monte Carlo simulations on the freely rotating chain, demonstrating accuracy across varying parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the general model and the need for a rigorous theory. It then develops the asymptotic expansion for the partition function and derives key observables like $\gamma(\eta)$. The theory is validated first analytically (freely jointed) and subsequently numerically (freely rotating chain).
why_interesting: While focused on classical polymer physics, the development of a systematic, rigorous asymptotic theory to handle complex degrees of freedom and stiffness parameters mirrors techniques used in quantum field theory or many-body systems where approximations are necessary.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The nondimensional longitudinal extension γ of an extensible freely jointed chain under an applied force η, using asymptotic (dashed) and analytically-exact (solid) methods, for varying nondimensional link stiffness κ.

FIG. 2. The nondimensional longitudinal extension γ of an extensible freely rotating chain under an applied force η, using asymptotic (dashed) and numerically-exact (solid) methods, for varying nondimensional link stiffness κ.

Fig. 2 compares the asymptotic approximation of the longitudinal extension γ(η) in Eq. (12) to the exact result in Eq. (8) as a function of the nondimensional force η across a range of nondimensional link stiffnesses κ [30]. Each case averages 10 billion Monte Carlo samples for every one of the 15 points along each curve, where the angle between consecutive link vectors is fixed at θb = 60◦

FIG. 3. The relative error e in the asymptotic theory as a function of the nondimensional link stiffness κ for both the extensible freely jointed chain (EFJC) and extensible freely rotating chain (EFRC).
